# Updated: 2026-05-16 08:18 GMT+8 by CLAUDE_CODE

PHASE: Phase 7 active — sixth Feature Update merged. Per-user 24h reset-request cap live: `authRouter.requestPasswordReset` now counts `PasswordResetToken` rows created for the matched user_id in the last 24h; if ≥5, returns ok silently and skips mint + email send (no-enumeration preserved). Closes the email-bomb gap where an attacker passing Turnstile could space requests past the 10/min per-email LRU and still flood a known user for hours.

LAST_DONE: 2026-05-16 Phase 7 #6 (Opus 4.7 direct, single-session, ~25K context — Tier 1). User picked option (h) from queued .whatsnext candidates. Delivered:
  (1) apps/web/src/server/trpc/routers/auth.ts — added 2 module constants (PER_USER_RESET_CAP=5, PER_USER_RESET_WINDOW_MS=24h) + a `passwordResetToken.count` query with 24h gte where clause + early-return on cap-met. Placement is AFTER the user lookup (otherwise the cap-check itself would leak user existence via timing/code-path differences).
  (2) apps/web/src/server/trpc/routers/auth.test.ts — added `count: vi.fn()` to mock factory, updated existing happy-path test to mock count=0, added 2 new tests: cap-engaged (count=5 → ok, no create, no send, count called with correct user_id + ~24h gte window) and one-under-cap (count=4 → still mints + sends). Updated unknown-email test to assert count is NOT called (preserves no-enumeration: lookup-misses skip the cap query entirely).
RED → GREEN proven inline: new cap-engaged test failed on initial run (router didn't call count); after implementation, all 15 auth.test.ts cases passed.

Direct execution chosen because the work is a tight backend addition with clear behavioral contract (count-then-cap) on top of an existing well-tested procedure — no schema change, no migration, no UI, no cross-file invariants beyond "preserve no-enumeration response shape".

PHASE_7_TICKET_6_NOTES:
  - Cap design rationale: 5/24h is a defence-in-depth layer on top of the existing per-email LRU (10/min). The LRU prevents rapid-fire bursts; the per-user 24h cap prevents sustained low-rate flooding against a known user (attacker spacing requests >6s apart could send 14k reset emails per day to one user without the cap). 5/day still allows legitimate edge cases (user forgets password, clicks reset, doesn't see email in spam, tries again — generous headroom). Cap is per-user (not per-email) because the matched user.id IS the right identity surface: the LRU already covers the email key; the DB count uses the canonical user identity.
  - Cap placement: AFTER user lookup. Placing it BEFORE would require a count keyed on email (cheap but defeats the design — we want to cap real users, not enumerate attempts). Placing it AFTER on a non-existent user is impossible because we short-circuit on `if (!user) return ok`. Net effect: unknown emails skip the cap query entirely (1 fewer DB call); known emails always run the cap query. This makes the cap-check leak-free for enumeration: an attacker can't distinguish "user doesn't exist" from "user exists but cap met" because both return the same shape with the same latency profile (lookup miss returns ok immediately; lookup hit always runs the cap count).
  - No-enumeration preserved: when the cap engages, the response is `{ ok: true }` — identical to (a) unknown email, (b) known email under cap. The only observable difference between cap-met and not-cap-met is whether an email lands in the user's inbox, which the attacker can't see (they don't control the inbox). This is the same posture security.md PRODUCTION ERROR HANDLING enforces: "If that email exists, a reset link has been sent."
  - Test coverage gain: auth.ts branches went 78.94% → 80.95% — the new cap-met branch is exercised by the cap-engaged test. Per-file gate at 75 still passes with 5.95% slack (was 3.94%). Global floor unchanged in % terms even though raw line counts went up (statements 63 → 67, lines 63 → 67) because the file totals also grew by the new code.
  - Migration: NONE. The existing PasswordResetToken table already has an index on user_id (line 11 of schema.prisma `@@index([user_id])`) which makes the count query O(log n) on user_id even without a composite [user_id, created_at] index. At 5/24h cap there will never be more than a handful of rows per user — performance is a non-issue. If a future cleanup job (candidate i) prunes consumed tokens >30 days, the count query stays fast forever.
  - Why not Prisma where + recent rows filter on findFirst+ orderBy? `count` is the right primitive: we don't need the row data, only the cardinality. count(where) is a single SELECT COUNT(*) and Prisma maps it to that exact query. findFirst with skip+limit would be misleading — that's "find Nth recent" not "are there at least 5".
